segments.s280-g33: This is an optative clause: جهان 'the world' is the topical subject, پیش شاه جهان 'before the King of the World' gives the locative/relative phrase, and بنده باد is the predicate with باد functioning as the archaic/poetic optative particle 'may it be'. Word order omits a copula and uses topicalization (object/scene first) typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s280-g34: همیشه 'always' is an adverb modifying the whole clause; دل پهلوان is a possessive noun phrase ('the heart of the champion') with the ezafe omitted as is common in verse; باد is again the optative 'may', and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful'. The clause therefore reads 'may the champion's heart always be happy.'
segments.s280-g1: ششم is a temporal/ordering adverb here ('then' / 'next') placed before the list of actors. The sequence بیژن گیو و رویین دمان names three participants; رویین دمان is an epithet (رویین 'iron‑hard' + دمان 'hem/skirt') functioning as a descriptive name. Word order is Topic/temporal → list of names (subject).
segments.s280-g2: به زه is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the string' placed before the verb for poetic word order. برنهادند is a past plural of نهادن with prefix بر- meaning 'placed/raised' (they set/raised onto the string). هر دو کمان is the object 'both bows' with هر دو = 'both'.
segments.s280-g3: چپ و راست گشتند uses coordinated direction adverbs 'left and right' with گشتند (past plural of گشتن) meaning 'turned' here; the following یک با دگر is an idiom 'one with the other' = 'at one another'. Word order (directions → verb → reciprocal phrase) is typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s280-g4: نبد is an archaic negative/predicative form meaning 'was not / there was not'. تیرشان is the definite/possessive object 'their arrow(s)'. از + کمان کارگر = 'from the effective/well‑working bow'; کارگر is used adjectivally 'effective'. The clause means literally 'their arrow was not from the effective bow' → 'not a one of their arrows came from the good bow.'
segments.s280-g5: به رومی عمود is a prepositional phrase 'toward the Roman column' (به + adjective رومی + noun عمود). آنگهی is a narrative adverb 'then/thereupon' placed before the personal name. پور گیو here functions as a compound name ('Pour‑Giv' / 'son of Giv') and is the sentence subject.
segments.s280-g6: همی گشت uses the prefixed particle همی (narrative/imperfective marker, 'kept / turned') with گشت (turned/wheeled). با + گرد + رویین نیو forms an instrument/adjunct: با 'with', گرد here 'around/about' (motion adverb), and رویین نیو is a poetic compound 'iron‑... (shaft/rod)' — the adjective رویین 'iron‑hard' modifies نیو (a short wooden/reed shaft in older usage), so the whole means he 'wheeled about with an iron shaft'.
segments.s280-g7: بر آوردگه is a locative phrase 'on/at the place of approach' (آوردگه = place of arrival/venue) with initial بر ('on/at'). The repeated بر بر is a poetic stacking of the preposition before the following object phrase. او دست یافت is an idiom: او = 'him', دست یافت (past of یافتن) = 'reached/laid hands on' → 'he seized him.'
segments.s280-g8: زمین را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker: 'the ground/earth (as object)'. بدرید is past tense 'tore' (active). و ... اندر شتافت links with 'and' to the verb شتافت (past of شتابیدن) with اندر 'into/within' — 'and he rushed in.' The sequence describes two successive actions.
segments.s280-g25: The phrase opens with the preposition به introducing a locative phrase; چنگ (claw) is the immediate object and اندرون (‘inside/within’) specifies location. شیر پیکر is a compound where شیر ('lion') modifies پیکر ('figure/body') and درفش ('standard/banner') is the head noun: syntactically the banner bears a lion‑shaped figure placed within a claw. The word order is characteristic of older/poetic Persian where modifiers can precede the head noun.
segments.s280-g26: میان is the preposition 'between' governing the coordinated noun phrase دیبه و رنگ خورده بنفش. دیبه = fine brocade; رنگ خورده + بنفش expresses a colored/ dyed purple fabric (رنگ خورده is a participial adjective 'having received color'). The coordination is straightforward: 'between X and Y.'
segments.s280-g27: چنینست is a fixed copular/explanatory phrase 'such is' or 'thus is.' کار جهان is a genitive phrase 'the work/affair of the world' and فریب functions as the predicate noun 'deception/trick.' Word order places the explanatory verb first, followed by the noun phrase and its predicate.
segments.s280-g28: پس here is a conjunctive particle 'therefore/so/then.' هر فرازی ('every ascent/lofty rise') is the topical NP, نهاده is a perfective/past participial verb 'has placed' or 'placed,' and نشیب ('descent') is the complement. The clause states a general truth in a classical/poetic past tense: after each rise a fall is set.
segments.s280-g29: وز here is an archaic/poetic form (functioning like از 'from'); آن جایگه ('that place/spot') is the noun phrase, and شد is the simple past/copular verb 'became/was (made)'. به جای نشان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'as a mark/in place as a sign' (به جای = 'in the place of' or 'as a...'). Overall: 'From that place a sign was set.'
segments.s280-g30: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near/close to' governing the following noun phrase آن نامور سرکشان. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the,' نامور is an adjective 'renowned,' and سرکشان is the plural noun (here meaning a group of proud/rebellious/chiefly persons); the whole phrase gives the location 'near those famed ones.'
segments.s280-g31: همی گفت is a classical/perfective iterative expression meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he continually said' (همی marks iterative/ongoing action). What follows is an optative blessing quoted without quotation marks: پیروزگر باد شاه = 'May the king be victorious' where باد is the optative particle 'may' and پیروزگر = 'victorious/conqueror' functions as predicate to شاه 'king.'
segments.s280-g32: This is the second wish parallel to the first, with the optative باد understood from the previous clause. همیشه is the adverb 'always,' سر پهلوان is the genitive phrase 'the champion's head,' and با کلاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with a helmet' (کلاه here = helmet). The clause is elliptical (no explicit verb because باد is implied): 'May the champion's head always be with a helmet.'
segments.s280-g9: زد is a simple past (3rd‑person) verb 'struck/hit'. از باد is an ablative simile/force phrase 'from/by the wind' (literally 'from wind') meaning 'like a blast of wind' or 'with wind‑force'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'on his head', with the enclitic ش = 'his'. رومی ستون is a noun phrase where رومی (modifier) qualifies ستون ('the Roman's shaft/column'); word order is Verb + adjuncts + affected NP, typical of classical Persian narration.
segments.s280-g10: فروریخت is a past intransitive verb 'collapsed/fell down' and here takes the preceding ستون as its subject (the column fell). از introduces the source 'from' (از ترگ او = 'from his ترگ'); ترگ here names the place/source from which the gore comes (an archaic/obscure word in this context). مغز و خون are the coordinated nouns 'brain and blood' that are presented as having poured out.
segments.s280-g11: به زین is a prepositional phrase 'onto/at the saddle' (به + زین). پلنگ is an epithet/name modifying زین (the ‘leopard’ saddle or a horse named 'Palang'). اندرون جان بداد is an idiomatic past construction: اندرون ('within/onto') + جان ('life') + بداد (poetic past of دادن 'gave') = 'gave up his life' (i.e. he died). Note: the lemmatized sense list labels بداد differently; here it functions as a past tense 'gave'.
segments.s280-g12: ز is the poetic/prepositional particle 'about/from' introducing the object of remembrance. پیران is the noun 'the elders' (object). ویسه is the actor/name (Viseh). بسی is an adverb 'much/very' modifying the verb کرد (past 'did'). یاد is the noun 'remembrance/memory' so بسی کرد یاد = 'he greatly remembered/paid much remembrance'.
segments.s280-g13: پس is a temporal/discourse adverb 'then/after'. از + پشت باره is an ablative phrase 'from behind / from the back burden/pack' (باره is an older noun here, contextually 'back/behind‑place'); درآمد is used as an intransitive verbal phrase 'came in/appeared/entered' and نگون is a predicate/adjective 'overthrown/cast down'. The clause narrates a sequence: then (he) came out from behind and was cast down.
segments.s280-g14: همه تن is the subject 'the whole body' (lit. 'all the body'). پر is a postpositive adjective meaning 'full of' (here 'covered with'); آهن names the thing he is full of — 'iron' (poetic for mail/armor). دهن پر ز خون is a parallel phrase 'the mouth full of blood' where دهن 'mouth' + پر 'full (of)' + ز خون 'with/from blood'. The structure uses repeated 'پر' to describe two complementary images.
segments.s280-g15: ز اسب اندر آمد is a simple narrative clause 'he dismounted / came down from the horse' where ز (ablative) + اسب 'horse' indicates source and اندر آمد (lit. 'entered inward') functions as 'alighted/dismounted' in poetic usage. سبک is an attributive adjective 'light (of step)' qualifying the following name/word بیژنا, which here functions as a proper name or epithet.
segments.s280-g16: مر is an enclitic-like datival/experiencer form 'to me' (used idiomatically: 'it seemed to me'). او را is the direct object 'him' (با را marking the pronoun). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the likeness/form/manner of' and آهرمنا names Ahriman (the Devil). Combined, the clause means 'he appeared to me in the form of Ahriman.'
segments.s280-g17: کمند (object) comes first and is followed by the past verb افگند — a typical SOV / V-final poetic order where 'he cast the lasso'. اندر is a preposition marking direction/place with افگند. The conjunction و links to the second clause بر زین کشید, where بر زین is a prepositional phrase 'onto/over the saddle' and کشید is a past tense verb here meaning 'drew/pulled' or 'slung/placed' (poetic past of کشیدن).
segments.s280-g18: نبد کس is an idiomatic negative existential: نبد (archaic past negative 'there was not') + کس 'person' → 'there was no one'. که introduces a relative clause 'who...'. تیمار رویین کشید is a relative predicate: رویین 'iron' modifies an implied armor/garment and کشید is used poetically in the past as 'drew on / put on / wore' (here 'put on iron [armor]').
segments.s280-g19: This clause uses an inverted order: مایه (capital/resource) is the theme that is affected, while برفت (past verb 'went/was lost') expresses the loss. از پی سود is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of profit' (از پی = 'after/in pursuit of', سود = 'profit'), and به باد completes the idiom به باد رفت (lit. 'went to the wind' = 'was lost').
segments.s280-g20: هنوز is an adverb 'still/yet' qualifying the following state. از جوانیش is a source phrase 'from/of his youth' (جوانی → youth). نابوده is a past/adjectival form meaning something like 'not undone/not ruined' (poetic/archaic usage here), and شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/merry' describing his state — together: 'still happy/unspoiled from his youth.'
segments.s280-g21: بر اسبش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his horse' (possessive suffix on اسب). به کردار is a locution 'in the manner/shape of', followed by پیلی (here an adjectival sense 'elephantine' or 'elephant-like') modifying how he fastened; ببست is the past verb 'he tied/fastened' (third-person past).
segments.s280-g22: گرفت is a simple past verb 'he seized/took'. آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon' linking the action, پالهنگش is the object with the possessive suffix 'his leopard', and به دست is an idiom 'in hand' meaning he took hold of it.
segments.s280-g23: عنان 'reins' is the direct object/topic and here is used with the verb بتافت (past of تافتن) which in poetic usage means 'flashed/whipped/moved quickly' — i.e., he jerked or brandished the reins. هیون تگاور is a compound attributive phrase (هیون 'beast/steed' + تگاور 'warrior/raider' or 'fierce rider') that qualifies the mount tied to those reins; the verb describes a sudden movement.
segments.s280-g24: وز (poetic variant of از) here functions as 'from' (archaic/poetic). آن جایگه = 'that place/spot'. سوی بالا is a directional phrase 'toward/upwards' and شتافت is the past verb 'hurried/rushed' (poetic past of شتافتن) — altogether: 'from that place he rushed upward.'

sentences.s280-s17: May the world be a servant before the King of the World; may the champion's heart always be joyful.
sentences.s280-s1: Then Bijan, Giv, and the iron‑hemmed (warrior) both drew their bows to the string.
sentences.s280-s2: They turned left and right at one another; not a single arrow of theirs came from the effective bow.
sentences.s280-s3: Toward the Roman column then Pour‑Giv turned, and he wheeled about with an iron shaft.
sentences.s280-s4: At the place of approach he laid hands on him; he tore up the ground and rushed in.
sentences.s280-s13: On the standard a lion‑shaped figure was set within a claw, between brocade and purple‑dyed cloth.
sentences.s280-s14: Such is the world's deceit; after every ascent a descent is placed.
sentences.s280-s15: From that spot a token/sign was set in place, near that famed band (of chiefs).
sentences.s280-s16: He kept saying, 'May the king be victorious; may the champion's head always be with a helmet.'
sentences.s280-s5: A Roman shaft struck his head like a blast of wind; the column collapsed, and from his skull his brain and blood spilled out.
sentences.s280-s6: He gave up his life on that leopard's saddle; Viseh much remembered the elders.
sentences.s280-s7: Then he came out from behind and was cast down; his whole body was covered with iron, his mouth full of blood.
sentences.s280-s8: Light‑footed Bijna dismounted from the horse; he appeared to me in the likeness of Ahriman.
sentences.s280-s9: He cast the lasso and drew it over the saddle; there was no one who had put on (worn) iron armor.
sentences.s280-s10: His capital was blown away in pursuit of profit; yet he remained merry as in his youth.
sentences.s280-s11: He fastened it to his horse in elephant-like fashion; then he seized his leopard in hand.
sentences.s280-s12: He snapped the reins of the war-steed and, from that spot, rushed upward.
